question.text: Scenario: Mr. Rahul Mehta, aged 35, earns Rs. 1,20,000 per month (net take-home). He has the following outstanding debts: (1) Home loan: Rs. 35 lakh outstanding at 8.5% p.a., EMI Rs. 32,000, remaining tenure 15 years; (2) Car loan: Rs. 4,50,000 outstanding at 11% p.a., EMI Rs. 10,000, remaining tenure 5 years; (3) Personal loan: Rs. 2,00,000 outstanding at 16% p.a., EMI Rs. 6,500, remaining tenure 3 years; (4) Credit card outstanding: Rs. 80,000 at 42% p.a. (revolving credit), minimum payment Rs. 4,000/month. His total monthly expenses (excluding debt servicing) are Rs. 45,000. He has recently received an annual bonus of Rs. 3,00,000 and wants advice on how to deploy it. His savings bank balance is Rs. 1,50,000 and he has Rs. 2,00,000 in a liquid mutual fund. Question 2 of 5: Using the avalanche strategy, in what order should Rahul prioritize debt repayment from the bonus?

answer.text: Home loan → Car loan → Personal loan → Credit card
Credit card (42%) → Personal loan (16%) → Car loan (11%) → Home loan (8.5%)
Personal loan → Credit card → Car loan → Home loan
Car loan → Personal loan → Credit card → Home loan

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Avalanche strategy = highest interest rate first. Order: Credit card (42%) → Personal loan (16%) → Car loan (11%) → Home loan (8.5%). The credit card's 42% effective rate makes it the most expensive and should be eliminated first. **The Trap:** The home loan is the largest balance but has the lowest rate. Paying it first would be the snowball-reverse (least efficient) approach. **Cross-Exam Context:** Avalanche debt repayment strategy is specifically tested in X-A (Ch 4). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Credit card revolving debt at 42% is a financial emergency — any available funds should go here first. The interest savings are immediate and massive.
